coredata.dc:title: One-step solvothermal synthesis of Na-doped carbon quantum dots and concomitant photochromic small molecules
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Yitong
Zhang, Danyang
Ren, Guozhu
Zhou, Shihong
Tian, Long
Zhou, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Hydrothermal method is a common way to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CQDs), but perplexing by-products are non-negligible shortcoming. The small molecules associated with CQDs synthesis have drawn more attentions because of their luminescent property. In this work, Na-doped CQDs with excellent photoluminescent property as well as small molecules 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene with photochromism characteristic were prepared based on p-phenylenediamine and sodium hydroxide via one-step solvothermal method. The photoluminescence of as-synthesized Na-doped CQDs is tremendously influenced by H+ ions, and CQDs-O- Na+ structure existing in Na-doped CQDs was verified. At the same time, photochromism which was owing to by-product 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene with photochromic was observed and investigated. The absorption spectrum of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene overlaps markedly with the photoluminescence spectrum of Na-doped CQDs and thus result in fluorescence internal filtering effect, by which the fluorescence causes interesting photochromism phenomenon. Furthermore, compared with conventional synthetic route of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene, this method has the advantages of simplicity and metal-free.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Na-doped
Photoluminescence property
Energy transfer
Photochromism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The structures and luminescent properties of obtained CQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research was funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 62174160) and CAS-Croucher Funding Scheme for Joint Laboratories: PolyU-CIAC Joint Laboratory (121522KYSB20200040).

The authors are grateful for the financial aid from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 62174160), the Young Scholar of Regional Development, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant No. 2022-028), and CAS-Croucher Funding Scheme for Joint Laboratories: PolyU-CIAC Joint Laboratory (121522KYSB20200040).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of Na-doped CQDs with size distribution. (b) XRD pattern of Na-doped CQDs. (c) Full scan XPS spectrum of Na-doped CQDs. (d-f) High resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s of Na-doped CQDs.

(a) Excitation and emission spectra of Na-doped CQDs. (b) Excitation spectra of Na-doped CQDs in different pH#. (c) Emission spectra of Na-doped CQDs in different pH# under 300 nm excitation light. (d) Excitation spectra of Na-doped CQDs in different pOH#. (e) Emission spectra of Na-doped CQDs in different pOH# under 300 nm excitation light. (f) FT-IR spectra of Na-doped CQDs before and after adding H+.
Conversion process of Na-doped CQDs after the adding of H+.

The images of sample before (a), being (b) and after (c) irradiated by UV lamp. (d) UV–Vis absorption spectra of synthesized sample before and after irradiated by UV lamp. (e) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the by-product before and after irradiated by UV lamp.

(a) The FT-IR spectra of the by-product and 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene. (b) The proposed photoisomerization mechanism of photochromic phenomenon. (c) The excitation (black line), emission (red and blue line) spectra of Na-doped CQDs and absorption (green line) spectrum of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene. (d) The emission spectra of Na-doped CQDs with 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene of 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 × 10-4 mmol/mL, respectively, under 300 nm excitation light. (e) The emission spectra of Na-doped CQDs with 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene of 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 × 10-4 mmol/mL, respectively, under 395 nm excitation light.
The synthetic route of Na-doped CQDs.

head.ce:title: One-step solvothermal synthesis of Na-doped carbon quantum dots and concomitant photochromic small molecules

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Resource Utilization

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: One-step solvothermal synthesis of Na-doped carbon quantum dots and concomitant photochromic small molecules.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrothermal method is a common way to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CQDs), but perplexing by-products are non-negligible shortcoming. The small molecules associated with CQDs synthesis have drawn more attentions because of their luminescent property. In this work, Na-doped CQDs with excellent photoluminescent property as well as small molecules 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene with photochromism characteristic were prepared based on p-phenylenediamine and sodium hydroxide via one-step solvothermal method. The photoluminescence of as-synthesized Na-doped CQDs is tremendously influenced by H+ ions, and CQDs-O- Na+ structure existing in Na-doped CQDs was verified. At the same time, photochromism which was owing to by-product 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene with photochromic was observed and investigated. The absorption spectrum of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene overlaps markedly with the photoluminescence spectrum of Na-doped CQDs and thus result in fluorescence internal filtering effect, by which the fluorescence causes interesting photochromism phenomenon. Furthermore, compared with conventional synthetic route of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene, this method has the advantages of simplicity and metal-free.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: For a long time, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, have widely attracted attentions for their unique and excellent luminescent properties. In general, CQDs are considered as carbon nanoparticles with less than 10 nm in diameter [1,2]. Compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs have numerous preponderances, such as lower toxicity and cost, more environmentally friendly, and easier to prepare. Because of that, many researchers have devoted to obtain CQDs with high performance in different application scenarios [3–7]. Up to now, CQDs with high quantum yield have been synthesized and utilized in light-emitting diodes [8–12]. At the same time, the carbon dots of biomass sources has gradually attracted a lot of attention [13–17]. Because of low toxicity and excellent luminescent property, it can also be exploited in bioimaging and sensing [18–21].

Recently, the synthesis, modification and application of CQDs are in full swing. However, there is a lot of uncertainty in synthesis processes, and the luminescence mechanism of CQDs has not been clearly explained. The results show that the structure of carbon dots is related to the luminescence properties, which is closely related to synthesis method and carbon source. Heteroatom doping is one of the widely studied modification methods [22,23]. For instance, heteroatom doping (such as N, O, P, S and B) helps to adjust the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), and thus has unique effect on the photoluminescence (PL) properties of CQDs [24–29]. Recent investigations have demonstrated that Na-doped CQDs exhibit efficient blue emission, which was explained as the influence of Na-O defect [30]. Phenylenediamine has a high carbon content, and the N element in amino group is easy to produce N-doped carbon dots, so the prepared sample has many unique properties. Hani et al. prepared Carbon dots for metal-ion detection using phenylenediamine [31]. Seung et al. used phenylenediamine as carbon source to prepare the multicolor emitting N-doped carbon dots [32]. Meanwhile, the by-products in CQDs synthesis process have not received enough attention, and some by-products may have interesting properties but be ignored.

In this work, we prepared Na-doped CQDs via solvothermal method using p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) as precursors. Interesting photochromic phenomenon was observed, and the effects of Na+ doping on luminescent properties of CQDs were studied in detail. The obtained Na-doped CQDs exhibited impressed H+-responsive luminescent phenomenon, which was interpreted as the change of surface group. The deep mechanism of photochromism phenomenon was experimentally explored and interpreted as the fluorescence internal filtering effect between Na-doped CQDs and small molecule by-product 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene.

NaOH was obtained from Beijing Chemical Works. Ethanol and concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were obtained from Xilong Scientific. p-PD, o-PD and m-PD were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene was obtained from Aladdin Corporation.

NaOH (0.2 g) and p-PD (0.4 g) were dissolved into ethanol (10 mL). The solution was transferred into polytetrafluoroethylene lined autoclave (25 mL), and then heated at 160 °C for 10 h. After that, the autoclave was naturally cooled down to room temperature. The crude products were centrifuged and then filtered using 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove insoluble matters. Finally, samples were dialyzed to neutral.
All the samples were tested in ethanol solution with the CQDs concentration of 0.05 mg/mL.

To adjust the concentrations of H+ and OH–, the ethanol solutions of H2SO4 and NaOH were prepared. The concentration of H2SO4 solution was 5 × 10-2, 5 × 10-3, 5 × 10-4, 5 × 10-5, 5 × 10-6 and 5 × 10-7 mol/L, denoted as pH#=1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 respectively. Similarly, the concentration of NaOH solution was 1 × 10-1, 1 × 10-2, 1 × 10-3, 1 × 10-4, 1 × 10-5 and 1 × 10-6 mol/L, denoted as pOH#=1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 respectively.
To reduce the interference of impurity, the commercial 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene with high purity was used in this work.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was taken with Transmission electron microscopy TECNAI G2. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was collected on Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer Thermoescalab 250XI. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected using Fourier transform infrared spectrometer Nicolet-6700 and Bruker VERTEX70 using the KBr pellet technique. Zeta potentials were investigated by using Zetasizer Nano ZS90 nanoparticle size potentiometer from Malvern Instruments ltd., UK. Mass spectra (MS) were recorded on the mass spectrometer Quattro Premier XE. Element analysis was carried out in a Vario EL cube elemental analyzer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were collected using Ultraviolet and visible spectrophotometer Persee TU-1901. Fluorescent emission spectra were recorded on Steady state and time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer Edinburgh instruments FLSP-920. 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were obtained from the AVANCE III HD 500 liquid high-resolution superconducting NMR spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structures and luminescent properties of obtained CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 shows the schematic illustration for the synthesis process of Na-doped CQDs. The obtained Na-doped CQDs can be clearly distinguished in the TEM image (Fig. 1 a), and the diameters are about 2 nm, which are consistent with the profile of common carbon quantum dots. As shown in Fig. 1b, there is a broad peak centered at about 25° in the XRD pattern, which is similar to that 26° of the graphite (002) plane [33]. It indicates that Na-doped CQDs with graphite phase as core have been successfully synthesized. The full XPS spectrum (Fig. 1c) of Na-doped CQDs shows four dominant peaks at 285.0, 399.2, 531.9 and 1071.0 eV, which correspond to C1s, N1s, O1s and Na1s, respectively. The peak of Na1s proves that element Na has been successfully doped into CQDs. The high resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1d) is divided into three peaks at 284.8, 286.0 and 288.1 eV, which can be attributed to C–C, C–O and C=O bands, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1e, there are C = N (398.1 eV), –NH2 (399.3 eV) and C–N (400.4 eV) in the Na-doped CQDs. It can be seen in Fig. 1f, the obtained CQDs contain C–O and C=O [34,35], which is in accordance with Fig. 1e. As shown in FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S2, in Supporting Information), the peaks at 3354 and 3187 cm−1 are originated from N–H stretching vibration, the peaks at 2924 and 2854 cm−1 are assigned to C-H stretching vibration of alkyl groups. While the peak at 1630 cm−1 is attributed to –NH2 angle-varying vibration [30,35,36]. Therefore, these results indicate the presence of C=O, C–O and –NH2 on the surface of Na-doped CQDs.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Na-doped CQDs (Fig. S3, in Supporting Information) shows strong absorption in 250 nm, which is arisen from the π-π* transitions of aromatic C=C bonds. Another shoulder peak at around 290 nm is assigned to n-π* electrons transition from the nonbonding orbital of surface group to the π* orbital of sp2 carbon domain. Additionally, an absorption plateau locating at around 420 nm is observed in the absorption spectrum, which is due to the surface state [37–39]. The PL spectra of Na-doped CQDs are presented in Fig. 2 a. The emission of Na-doped CQDs peaks at 422 and 500 nm when the excitation wavelengths are 300 and 395 nm, respectively.

To further investigate the surface functional groups of Na-doped CQDs, we performed the special experiment by adding H+ and OH– ions with different concentrations into the obtained CQDs solution. Considering that the experiment was conducted in ethanol solution, pH# and pOH# were used to represent the concentrations of H+ and OH–, respectively. With decreasing pH# from 5 to 4, as shown in Fig. 2b and Fig. 2c, new peaks appear in excitation and emission spectra. When the excitation wavelength is around 425 nm, a new peak centered at 500 nm appears in the emission spectra of Na-doped CQDs solution with pH# less than 5. The FT-IR spectrum shows an O-H vibration absorption peak at around 3400 cm−1 after adding H+ ions (Fig. 2f), which is not observed before. Simultaneously, zeta potential test results (Fig. S4a, in Supporting Information) exhibit that the zeta potential of Na-doped CQDs increases from −13.3 to + 25.3 mV with decreasing pH# from 5 to 4. These results indicate that the surface groups of obtained Na-doped CQDs have been changed. Moreover, excitation and emission wavelength have no marked change after the addition of different concentrations of OH– (Fig. 2d and e). The decrease in fluorescence intensity is likely due to the agglomeration of CQDs, which is illustrated by the reduction of the Zeta value (Fig. S4b).

Based on the aforementioned results, we propose that there is CQDs-O- Na+ in the surface of Na-doped CQDs. The variation of PL spectra is probably due to the conversion of CQDs-O- Na+ to CQDs-O-H after the adding of H+ with concentration greater than 10-5 mol/L, which causes the change of surface state. And the high positive value of zeta potential might come from protonated amine groups, which presumably contribute to spectral variation [40]. It has been demonstrated by Jeon’s group that different surface groups can strongly influence the energy level of CQDs [41]. The absorption spectra of Na-doped CQDs are illustrated in Fig. S5, in the Supporting Information. The absorption band at around 420 nm decreases while a new band increases at 510 nm with increasing H+ concentration, which is related to the decrease of surface energy gap, resulting in longer emission wavelength. So, as depicted in Fig. 3 , the surface state strongly influences the optical properties of Na-doped CQDs.

Interestingly, photochromic phenomenon was found in the sample without dialyzer purification. As shown in Fig. 4 a–c, color of sample changes from yellow to orange after exposed at UV light. When light source is removed, the color of sample returns to yellow in tens of seconds, which means the photochromic process is reversible. The phenomenon has rarely been reported before. In this case, we suppose that it might come from Na-doped CQDs or small molecule by-products. To explore the possible causes of its photochromic property, dialysis of obtained sample was performed to remove small molecules. After that, the photochromic phenomenon vanished. Therefore, small molecule by-products may be responsible for this phenomenon. According to previous reports, small molecule by-products have great influence on luminescent properties of CQDs [42]. The by-product was separated via column chromatography using petroleum ether and ethyl acetate as eluent agent.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the sample were measured and shown in Fig. 4d, which display that most absorption peaks centered at 400 nm. After irradiated by UV lamp, the absorption intensity decreases, and another absorption band between 500 and 600 nm appears. The absorption spectrum of the by-product (Fig. 4e) matches well with Fig. 4d, which suggests that by-product is indeed responsible for the photochromic phenomenon.

To determine the molecular structure of by-product, FT-IR spectrum was tested and presented in Fig. 5 a. The peaks at 1507, 1590 and 1617 cm−1 are assigned to the skeleton vibration of benzene moiety, while the peak at 1143 cm−1 is assigned to C-H in-plane bending vibration in aromatic ring. The peak at 837 cm−1 is attributed to outer bending vibration of aromatic ring, which indicating the existence of para-orientated substitution. The peaks at 3377 and 3477 cm−1 are attributed to N-H stretching vibration of aromatic primary amine, and the peaks at 1300 and 1237 cm−1 are recognized as C-N stretching vibration in aromatic primary amine. As presented in Fig. S6, in the Supporting Information, the MS indicates that its relative molecular mass is 212. 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were measured and given in Fig. S7 and Fig. S8, in the Supporting Information. Elemental analysis shows that it contains nitrogen 24.28 %, carbon 67.36 %, and hydrogen 5.96 %. Based on these testing, the by-product is determined to be 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene, which causes the photochromic phenomenon due to its photoisomerization [43–46], as shown in Fig. 5b. By comparing our experimental results with the spectra of commercial material, the presence of by-product is further confirmed. The synthesis yield of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene in this work is about 10 %. Furthermore, compared with previously reported synthesis routes, this method has the advantages of easy operation and no need of metal catalyst [47–49].

Notably, as shown in Fig. 5c, the absorption spectrum of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene overlaps with the PL spectrum of Na-doped CQDs to a great extent, suggesting that there might be energy transfer from Na-doped CQDs to 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene. It can be seen from Fig. S9, in the Supporting Information, under the same condition, fluorescence of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene is very weak, because part energy absorbed by 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene is consumed by the non-radiative transition process of photoisomerization (Fig. 5b). The PL spectra of Na-doped CQDs with different concentrations of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene were measured and given in Fig. 5d and e. As shown in Fig. 5d, under 300 nm excitation light, with increasing concentration of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene, fluorescence intensity of Na-doped CQDs significantly decreases especially at 350 to 450 nm, which is consistent with the absorption spectrum of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene (Fig. 4d). The fluorescence intensity from 500 to 600 nm also decreases with increasing concentration of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene, which is related to the broadening absorption band of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene, suggesting that photoisomerization occur during this process. On the other hand, as depicted in Fig. 5e, with increasing concentration of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene, fluorescence intensity of Na-doped CQDs under 395 nm excitation also decreases significantly. This can be attributed to the fact that the excitation wavelength derived from Na-doped CQDs matches well with the absorption wavelength of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene, leading to a competition between them, and thus resulting in decreased fluorescence emission intensity of Na-doped CQDs.

Based on above results, it is presented that Na-doped CQDs and 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene have strong fluorescence inner filter effect (IFE) in this work. The practical application of IFE system can be mainly divided into two types of fluorescence quenching. One is the fluorophore’s emission band matches with the absorber’s absorption, leading to decreased emission intensity. The other is that the excitation spectrum of fluorophore is very similar to the absorption spectrum of absorber, leading to radiation shielding [50,51]. In this work, not only the fluorescence emission from Na-doped CQDs is absorbed by 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene under excitation of 300 nm light, but also the excitation of Na-doped CQDs is consistent with the absorption of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene under 395 nm light. Thus, photochromic phenomenon of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene occur through IFE between Na-doped CQDs and 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene. According to Fig. S10, in the Supporting Information, Na-doped CQDs give very similar emission at different excitation wavelengths. That is to say, the interaction of Na-doped CQDs and 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene can be used to achieve photochromic effects at more light source with different wavelengths. Moreover, the emission light of Na-doped CQDs can caused photochromic effect, which provides a novel application of CQDs.

In this work, Na-doped CQDs were synthesized and investigated via one-step solvothermal synthesis. Experimental results showed that Na existed in this obtained CQDs with the form of CQDs-O- Na+. After adding H+, CQDs-O- Na+ was converted to be CQDs-O-H, and the change of surface state significantly influenced the PL spectrum of Na-doped CQDs. At the same time, the by-product of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene with photoisomerization property was concomitantly produced during synthesis process. The emission spectrum of Na-doped CQDs overlaps markedly with the absorption spectrum of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene, resulting in obvious IFE. Through IFE, the emission of Na-doped CQDs could be converted to that of 4,4′-diaminoazobenzene, thus resulting in interesting photochromic phenomenon.
Not applicable.
Not applicable.
The research was funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 62174160) and CAS-Croucher Funding Scheme for Joint Laboratories: PolyU-CIAC Joint Laboratory (121522KYSB20200040).

Yitong Sun: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Danyang Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Guozhu Ren: Formal analysis. Shihong Zhou: Investigation. Long Tian: Investigation. Liang Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial aid from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 62174160), the Young Scholar of Regional Development, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant No. 2022-028), and CAS-Croucher Funding Scheme for Joint Laboratories: PolyU-CIAC Joint Laboratory (121522KYSB20200040).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2024.141114.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): current state and future perspective

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable photoacoustic and fluorescence imaging of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-energy short-wave blue light conversion films via carbon quantum dots for preventing retinal photochemical damage

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Scalable synthesis of green fluorescent carbon dot powders with unprecedented efficiency

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bright multicolor bandgap fluorescent carbon quantum dots for electroluminescent light-emitting diodes
